item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I have no objections to this!

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: author approved edits

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This SCP mentions the Global Occult Coalition. I propose linking it to the hub.

Editing this:

The Global Occult Coalition and the Foundation have agreed to collaborate for the sole purpose of constructing a large-scale memetic measure that will counteract SCP-2574's effects on global psychology,

To this:

The Global Occult Coalition and the Foundation have agreed to collaborate for the sole purpose of constructing a large-scale memetic measure that will counteract SCP-2574's effects on global psychology,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, W.B Yeats is now a lion…+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's So Meta, Even This Acronym!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm largely with sirpudding and Kalinin. Having not read the poem, I could still tell you were drawing details from something else without really interpreting them, and taken as a literal object, they're just not that interesting and don't hang together in a sensible way.

Yeats being possibly trapped inside it didn't help this; it came off as a semi-ironic comeuppance, but Yeats didn't seem to do anything to deserve it and the comeuppance is too heavy-handed. It feels like something you'd get in a Simpsons monkey paw parody. I understand that it wasn't your intention to consign Yeats to damnation for writing a poem (at least I hope) and this was more a result of the state of humanity, but that's the level of silliness going on here.

I like the build-up and the global reactions and scenario, but the anomaly (including the WWI manifestations and other physical, if not mental/social effects) just felt like a confusing mishmash of elements. I just couldn't get invested in it.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dig it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2574 will reach its destination in approximately 1 month.

HARD

CORE

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I may have skewed too subtly on this - as there is the question of - why did it awaken now? It's implied in both the journal and the footnote that this is, in fact, the case, though I did, after reading this, remove a footnote that said the theory was summarily disproved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What Magnus stated above was, in fact, my intent with this, but it is still on me that the interpretation didn't come through strongly enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would buy that more if the sphinx weren't traveling in a direct path toward a fixed location in a predictable manner. I also tend to give a certain amount of credence to the Foundation's summation of things, as for a variety of reasons I assume that they have a certain baseline level of competence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I may have read it differently, but from what I got out of it, while the Foundation assumes this creature's movement causes the violence and conflict, it's that the violence and conflict of man propel this creature's movement in truth.

It's not the spiral pattern causing violence, it's the violence causing the spiral pattern.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If nothing else, this was a good excuse to read The Second Coming again, which I had not done in years.

I have two major problems here. The first is the silliness of making abstract, symbolic imagery be their literal, physical counterparts for the purposes of the story. The sphinx in Yeats' mind in the poem is a powerful symbol of something enduring (and not really pleasant) in the spirit of humanity, an association that would have been even more mystical in the context of Spiritualism and occultism, which was in vogue at the time and in which Yeats had a pronounced interest. Making this monstrous, immortal aspect of humanity's nature a literal monster that literally travels towards a place just doesn't work for me here. Something about a sphinx shuffling along while birds peck at it, destroying stuff like a poetry Godzilla, just is not resonating with me as something to be taken seriously.

The second is that this works against your source material by attributing the violence of humanity, at least in part, to external forces. Yeats' poem is about something inexplicable and terrible that resides within all of us, that is such a fundamental part of us that it beats out things like religion. The Second Coming is a great example of poetry; it tells us immediate truths about ourselves not because it makes an argument, but because it uses language to speak to us on a deeper level, at which things like his image of the Sphinx exist. This article is in fact undermining that truth on a certain level by attributing brutality and violence to external forces. Whatever scenario you've got cooked up, whatever is documented in this file, it doesn't really compare to the blood-dimmed tide drowning humanity's innocence.

I like that you took some actual art as inspiration for something, and I respect the attempt at making it work. I think my problem is that once you chose The Second Coming as your starting point, you sort of hewed a little too closely to the same thematic ground it was covering, and I don't know a single one of us that is going to compare favorably to Yeats when it comes to that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that'd work. 'Surge' could also work, if you wanted to keep the same imagery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed the bit about the border poll. Would "becoming more prominent/widespread" be a better phrasing for the nationalism part?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is known.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoy the historical aspect of this one, I wish there was more. The entity serves as a nice framing device for everything else you do in the article. Although in some places it feels like it might come apart from its sheer frenetic nature, the end holds it together well enough. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was intense. Easy +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I always have a soft spot for Irish related SCPs and this is well written to boot. +1 from me.

+ Some small nitpicks and comments
- hide

…more pronounced in countries involved in either World War I or the Irish War of Independence.

Countries involved in the Irish War of Independence would be the UK and Ireland, wouldn't it? Both of whom were involved in WWI?

Irish-English conflict begins to grow more violent

Well, Ireland's fucked.

a resurgence in Northern Irish nationalism is seen

I'm not sure if resurgence is the right word here, I mean it's never gone away or died or anything.

Multiple secession referendums have been proposed and are awaiting review by Parliament.

Assuming this is about Northern Ireland joining the Republic

  1. It'd most likely be called a border poll
  2. It wouldn't have to be approved by Parliament actually. If Northern Ireland's Secretary of State ever thinks it's likely that majority of voters in Northern Ireland would vote to leave the UK and form a part of a united Ireland, they can hold a poll. After that, (assuming they vote to leave) the Secretary of State "shall lay before Parliament such proposals to give effect to that wish as may be agreed between Her Majesty’s Government in the United Kingdom and the Government of Ireland." So says the Good Friday agreement anyway

Anyway, I enjoyed reading it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Which is the poem that this article is a literalization of, if that wasn't obvious.

The thing slouching toward Bethlehem in TL333s' subconscious is a "rough beast" which is the source of the title of the featured tale…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
bwahahahamarpahahahaha

For those wondering, "slouches toward Bethlehem" is from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Second_Coming_(poem)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
heya niles hows it goin
i am here with the Crits

I've got a bit of a killer headache1 so I'm skimming a little, apologies - bug me in IRC to go over it again tomorrow or something, if you'd like.

Knowledge of SCP-2574 is too widespread for amnestics to be an effective containment procedure. Populated areas plotted to be in SCP-2574's path are to be evacuated at least 1 week before SCP-2574 reaches the area.

I feel these sentences could be collapsed together; "Knowledge of SCP-2574 has spread to the point of rendering amnestics insufficient, and populated areas plotted to be in SCP-2574's path are to be evacuated at least 1 week before SCP-2574 reaches the area." Or similar.

SCP-2574 is a 12 meter-tall leonine creature composed of sandstone and leonine smooth muscle tissue.

Repetition of leonine - intentional? Looks a little sloppy.

SCP-2574 is surrounded at all times by a flock of birds of prey.

What birds, how many, do they change by region, etc?

SCP-2574 constantly regenerates from the damage done by SCP-2574-1.

How quickly, and by what method (or is it anomalous spacemagicks) :p

Currently, SCP-2574 is estimated to have caused 25.3 million US dollars in damages, 112 deaths, and 625 injuries.

Can probably be shortened to USD for brevity.

Hallucinations of aerial attacks, abnormal chemicals, manmade landforms, and a variety of weaponry have all been observed in the wake of SCP-2574. SCP-2574 also causes temporal anomalies, producing historically accurate hallucinations of combat during both World War I and the Irish War of Independence, and causing both temporary and permanent manifestations of persons involved in either conflict.

Hallucinations and actual physical things happening are quite different;2 it's not really clear which is going on here. If you need advice on writing hallucinations, hit me up.

3. Due to the nature of SCP-2574, it has been conjectured that SCP-2574 was not the source of these effects, but was rather responding to them. Evidence for this theory comes from the occurrence of several international conflicts days before SCP-001's first appearance.

Missed an -001 there :p

i am here with the Crits

Overall: 7/10, updooted, enjoyed. I think the documents could do with a different layout (maybe tabs? or a table?) to help with consumption of them. I could throw together a mockup in a sandbox, if you want.

Footnotes
1. Gave blood today. Woo, helping!
2. Source: I have 'em. Hallucinations, not materializations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
because I got the phrase stuck in my head

I suspect this month's featured tale may be the culprit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mainly the device of taking Yeats' symbolism and making it literal just doesn't work for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I can't speak for Sirens or sirpudding, but for me its the fact that the base anomaly boils down to a giant unstoppable monster. Granted, you do a pretty solid job of taking the metaphorical creature from the poem and describing it in a literal sense. The imagery is there and succeeds in getting across what the monster looks like. However, in the end it is still just a giant monster. This wiki is chock full of those. So that aspect of this article doesn't shine as bright as the rest of it.

Honestly, I personally would have found it far more entertaining if the monster was the zombie corpse of Yeats, with all the same shenanigans going down as it walks from Israel to Sarajevo, but that is just me, and that would require a major rewrite.

All that being said, I still think its worth a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's creepy, because I just looked up "slouches toward Bethlehem" before coming here today, because I got the phrase stuck in my head and couldn't remember where it came from.

And this thing. What the hell? The birds of prey attacking it are probably my favorite bit, it's just such a striking image. There are spots that could stand smoothing out, but as this is, it's worth an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm. I personally really like the imagery of this creature and I thought it meshed well with what it caused, and the point of the article in this form was always going to be watching the world collapse around it. What might make it more interesting, in your eyes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I struggled with the concept of a Broken Masquerade scenario where none of the ramifications of anomalies made public were discussed, but reading the communication with various governments and the overall quality of the idea overcame my qualms.

+1, thanks for reminding me of a great Yeats poem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Presumably World War III to be fought with unknown weapons, followed by WWIV to be fought with sticks and stones, followed by WWV to be fought with phased plasma rifles in the 40 watt range followed by WWW to be fought by professional wrestlers. It is known.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the implication that, provided the world still exists in some usable format after the rough beast is no more, the Foundation and the GOC will team up to ensure that nobody really remembers what happened. Worldwide devastation, with a plausible explanation; anyone with a sufficiently accurate memory of 2574 will be disregarded as a crank or quietly quieted.

All that will remain is a garbled account, written as fiction to entertain and inspire people who like that sort of thing.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You missed an 001 in the footnotes. >_>

This is great. Yeats is one of the few poets I actually like, and this is awesome. Makes me wonder what the fuck is going to happen when it does get to Sarajevo. Nothing good, I bet.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also going to agree with this. The end of the article is far more entertaining and interesting than the first half of it but I don't think that's enough for me to upvote. The world building is great, but otherwise, it's not doing anything for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I too am pretty conflicted here, the metaphorical merely made literal is seldom a good idea, and this starts like that but concludes more strongly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, so I'm torn on this one.

On one hand, I find the base anomaly, the lion human construct from the Yeats poem to be kind of dull. Its just comes across to me as another unstoppable monster, albeit one based on a pretty neat poem.

On the other hand, the supporting documentation for this article is really enjoyable. I found the table in particular to be interesting, especially watching the anomalous effects amp up as it gets closer to Sarajevo, while the political stability of the world in turn disintegrates. The historical documentation, and the excerpts from the media/GOC and SCPF communications also intrigued me, and am genuinely interested in what happens when the beast finally gets from Point A to Point B.

I'm going to err on the side of an upvote. The monster itself might be lackluster, but that is some neat world building in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fucked up okay (it's gone now), no worries

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
FIX THIS ONE

hmm

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 001s are HARD, man. So you have this, instead. Thank to everyone who looked at this, which is too long a list to replicate here, and I will inevitably miss people, but special thanks to minmin, Jekeled, Conwell, A Random Day, Magnus, weryllium, Feline, shaggy, and…god. I don't even know.
